focus: Compare committed static indexing, a structured query store, on-demand DESIGN.md grep, and a layered hybrid using repository-native precedents and measured corpus costs
noveltyJustification: 4 of 6 findings were fully new and 2 partially extended iteration 1; resolving the stale manual-index contradiction added the 0.10 simplicity bonus, yielding 0.93.
keyQuestions: Which retrieval substrate, or layered combination of substrates, best balances relevance, determinism, runtime cost, context size, and maintenance?
What should the hub and each of the five modes consume, and when should a task reference one coherent real style versus synthesize selected attributes across several styles?
Which anti-slop rules and proof gates keep outputs distinctive rather than averaging the corpus into a generic middle?
What index build, query, refresh, validation, and corpus-change tooling is justified by the repository's existing patterns?
How should utilization strategies rank by leverage versus build cost after accounting for size, staleness, licensing, provenance, and extraction fidelity risks?
ruledOut: Static-manifest-only as the final relevance ranker
Queryable database as canonical source of truth
Broad on-demand grep as the default retrieval path
sourcesQueried: read-only Python corpus and in-memory retrieval benchmark output at 2026-07-18T09:40Z

text: Use a committed, generated, hash-checkable retrieval manifest as source of truth and keep SQLite/FTS/embeddings disposable and generation-bound.
The manual README count of 50 conflicts with 1,034 captured bundles and a 1,290-row crawl manifest; generated freshness metadata must replace hand-maintained retrieval inventory.
A 1,034-style compact prototype was 502,895 bytes and built in 171.1 ms, but title/heading metadata missed all three body-attribute queries.

In-memory SQLite/FTS ingested in 179.7 ms, used 29,093,888 page bytes, and answered BM25 tests in 0.054-0.203 ms; repository capability probes and fail-open behavior make it suitable as a derived accelerator.
On-demand full-file scans were fresh and deterministic but reread 20,378,783 bytes and took 32.71-33.35 ms/query without structured joins or stable rank.
sources: read-only Python full-file scan benchmark at 2026-07-18T09:40Z

text: Recommended flow is committed manifest, deterministic eligibility/provenance filters, lexical BM25, optional versioned semantic rerank, and mode-specific selective hydration.
